elements.sent-2411-280: Several known constructions relate initial degenerations of projective toric varieties and Grassmannians to regular subdivisions of appropriate point configurations.
elements.sent-2411-281: We define a general framework which allows for partial generalizations of these constructions to arbitrary projective schemes (as well as their very affine parts).
elements.sent-2411-282: We associate a point configuration $A$ with any homogeneous ideal $I$.
elements.sent-2411-283: We obtain upper and lower bounds on every initial ideal of $I$, defining them in terms of the regular subdivision of $A$ given by the same weight.
elements.sent-2411-284: Furthermore, both bounds are interpreted categorically via (co)limits over the face poset of the subdivision.
elements.sent-2411-285: We also investigate when these bounds are exact, showing that the respective weights form a subfan in the secondary fan of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-286: Initial ideals have their origins in computer algebra, specifically in the work of Buchberger, and play a central role in the area of Gr\"obner theory.

elements.sent-2411-730: In this section, we give a new infinite family of initial degenerations $\init_{w}\Gr(k,n)^{\circ}$ where the mediating morphism is an isomorphism, motivated by the class of inductively connected matroids as defined in \cite{LiwskiMohammadi .}
elements.sent-2411-731: We rapidly review some basic matroid theory.
elements.sent-2411-732: We follow the treatment in \cite{Oxley and refer the reader to this text for details.}
elements.sent-2411-733: A matroid (of rank $k$) is a pair $M = (\cE(M), \cB(M))$ where $\cE(M)$ is a finite set and $\cB(M)$ is a subset of $\binom{\cE(M) {k}$ satisfying the basis exchange axiom: for each pair of distinct $A, B\in \cB$ and $a\in A$, there is a $b\in B$ such that $(A\setminus \{a\}) \cup \{b\}$ lies in $\cB(M)$.}
elements.sent-2411-734: Here $\cE(M)$ is called the ground set of $M$, an element of $\cB(M)$ is called a basis of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-735: An independent set of $M$ is a subset of $\cE(M)$ that is contained in a basis of $M$; the set of independent sets of $M$ is denoted by $\cI(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-736: A circuit of $M$ is a minimal dependent set.
elements.sent-2411-737: The rank $\rk_{M (X)$ of $X\subset \cE(M)$ is the size of the largest independent subset of $X$, and the rank of $M$ is the rank of its ground set.}
elements.sent-2411-738: Given a subset $X\subset \cE(M)$, the restriction of $M$ to $X$ is the matroid, denoted by $M|X$, whose independent sets are \begin{equation}\cI(M|X) = \{ I \subseteq X \suchthat I\in \cI(M)\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-739: An element $e\in \cE(M)$ is a coloop of $M$ if it is contained in every basis of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-740: A flat of $M$ is a subset $F\subset \cE(M)$ such that $\rk_{M (F \cup \{e\}) = \rk_{M (F) + 1$ for each $e\in \cE(M)\setminus F$.}}
elements.sent-2411-741: A cyclic flat of $M$ is a flat $F$ such that $M|F$ has no coloops.

elements.sent-2411-777: In this short appendix, we describe how to obtain the point configuration $\cA(I)$ from an ideal $I$ using computational methods.
elements.sent-2411-778: For the purposes of this section, we set $E=\{1,\dots,m\}$ and $I\subset \bC[x_1,\ldots,x_m]=S$.
elements.sent-2411-779: Recall that $\cL(I) \subset \bR^{m}$ is the lineality space of $\Grob I$.
elements.sent-2411-780: We say that $p\in S$ is $\cL(I)$--homogeneous if it is homogeneous with respect to every $w\in\cL(I)$.
elements.sent-2411-781: While $I$ is generated by $\cL(I)$--homogeneous polynomials, an arbitrary generating set may contain elements that are not of this form.
elements.sent-2411-782: This issue is resolved by the following proposition.
elements.sent-2411-783: If $\cG$ is a reduced Gr\"obner basis of $I$ relative to a fixed (Artinian) monomial order $<$, then each $f\in \cG$ is $\cL(I)$--homogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-785: We may decompose $f$ into the sum of $\cL(I)$--homogeneous components, all of which lie in $I$.
elements.sent-2411-786: Choose such a component $g$ that does not contain the leading term $\init_< f$.
elements.sent-2411-787: The leading term $\init_< g$ lies in the initial ideal $\init_< I$ and must be divisible by the leading term of some other element of $\cG$, which contradicts reducedness.
elements.sent-2411-788: Now, given a reduced Gr\"obner basis $\cG=\{f_1,\dots,f_n\}$ of $I$, the computation of $\cL(I)$ reduces to finding a matrix kernel.
elements.sent-2411-789: Let $f_i$ contain $l_i$ nonzero monomials with exponent vectors $v^i_1,\dots,v^i_{l_i}\in\bZ_{\ge0 ^m$.}
elements.sent-2411-790: Let $N$ be a matrix with $m$ columns, whose rows are the $l_1+\dots+l_n-n$ vectors $v^i_1-v^i_j$ with $i\in[1,n]$ and $j\in[2,l_i]$.
elements.sent-2411-791: For $w\in\bR^m$, one has $w\in\cL(I)$ if and only if $Nw=0$.
elements.sent-2411-792: By our definition of $N$, the condition $Nw=0$ is equivalent to every $f_i$ being homogeneous with respect to $w$.
elements.sent-2411-793: Since $I$ is generated by $\cG$, we deduce that $w\in\cL(I)$ whenever $Nw=0$.
elements.sent-2411-794: Conversely, Proposition~\ref{prop:redGrob shows that if $w\in\cL(I)$, then $Nw=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-795: Given a matrix $N$ as in the above proposition, standard computer algebra tools let us find a matrix $M$ whose columns form a basis of $\ker N=\cL(I)$.
elements.sent-2411-796: The next statement allows us to pass to $\cA(I)$.
elements.sent-2411-797: Let $M$ be a real matrix with $m$ rows whose columns form a basis of $\cL(I)$.
elements.sent-2411-798: Each row of $M$ may be viewed as a point in $\cL(I)^*$, written in the dual basis.
elements.sent-2411-799: The obtained configuration of $m$ points in $\cL(I)^*$ is precisely $\cA(I)$.
elements.sent-2411-800: For $j\in[1,\dim\cL(I)]$, let $v_j\in\bR^m$ be the $j$th column of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-801: As in Subsection~\ref{subspaceconfig , for $i\in[1,m]$, let $u^{\cL(I) _i\in\cL(I)^*$ denote the restriction of the $i$th coordinate function to $\cL(I)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-802: The proposition asserts that $u^{\cL(I) _i(v_j)=M_{i,j}$ for all $i,j$, which is by construction.}
elements.sent-2411-803: To summarize, given an ideal $I$, the point configuration $\cA(I)$ can be found as follows.
elements.sent-2411-804: One first applies a Gr\"obner basis computation to obtain a reduced Gr\"obner basis $\cG$.
elements.sent-2411-805: Next, the kernel of the matrix $N$ considered in Proposition~\ref{prop:matrixN is computed.}
elements.sent-2411-806: The result will be a matrix whose columns form a basis of $\cL(I)$ and, by Proposition~\ref{prop:Afrommatrix , the rows of this matrix will form $\cA(I)$.}
